summary: These rules explain your right to see and get copies of your health information held by a covered entity. You can choose the format (including electronic, when the records are kept electronically) if it’s readily producible. Access must be provided in a timely way, at a convenient time/place or by mail. Only limited, reasonable, cost-based fees may be charged. If access is denied, you must receive a clear written explanation, information on what you still can access, and, if applicable, how to ask for a review.
key_points: You may inspect or get a copy (or both) of your protected health information in designated record sets.
If the same information exists in multiple places, it only needs to be produced once.
Form and format: you can get your information in the form/format you request if readily producible; otherwise, in a readable hard copy or another agreed format.

If records are kept electronically and you request an electronic copy, it must be provided in the requested electronic format if readily producible; otherwise, in a readable electronic format you and the covered entity agree on.
A summary of your information may be provided instead of full access if you agree.
Access must be provided in a timely manner, including arranging a convenient time/place to inspect or obtain a copy, or mailing it if you ask.

Fees must be reasonable and cost-based, and may include only: labor for copying (paper or electronic), supplies for the copy or electronic media if you request it on portable media, postage if mailed, and preparing a summary or explanation if you agree.
If access is denied in whole or in part, you should still get access to any other information that can be provided.
Any denial must be timely, in plain language, and include the reason for denial and, if applicable, your review rights and how to exercise them.
